title: Quick check: Image scanning and signing

questions.prompt: Where in a CI/CD pipeline should image vulnerability scanning happen?

choices.text: Multiple points, with CI as the highest-leverage. In CI: scan the built image; fail the build on Critical CVEs before push. In the registry: continuous scans of pushed images so new CVEs against existing images alert. At deploy: admission controllers verify signatures and re-check scan status. At runtime: monitor for new CVEs against deployed images. CI scanning is the highest-impact point because it stops vulnerable images from ever reaching production. Tune severity thresholds (Critical-only vs Critical+High) to balance security with alert noise. Allowlist specific CVEs you've assessed and accepted.

questions.explanation: Scan in CI to fail fast; scan in registry for ongoing visibility; verify at deploy. Multi-point defense.

questions.prompt: A team wants to verify only signed images can deploy to production. How?

choices.text: Sign images with Cosign at build time; verify signatures at deploy via an admission controller (Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper, cosigned). The CI pipeline signs images after building and pushing; the Kubernetes admission controller blocks Pods whose image manifests aren't signed by an approved key. Combined with image-pull policies that require digest references (not floating tags), you get strong supply-chain integrity: only images built by your CI, signed by your key, can run. SLSA-style supply chain protection. Sigstore's ecosystem (Cosign, Rekor, Fulcio) is the modern standard.

questions.explanation: Cosign for signing + admission controller for verification. Standard supply-chain integrity pattern.
